answer:
**In Buddhist Old Javanese (Kawi) texts**, *dhyāna* (ध्यान) is prominently featured in *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, a Mahāyāna tantric initiation and conduct manual. It is the fifth of the *ṣaṭpāramitā* (Six Perfections): *dāna* (giving), *śīla* (morality), *kṣānti* (patience), *vīrya* (effort), *dhyāna* (meditation), and *prajñā* (wisdom).[^1] The *dhyānapāramitā* (Perfection of Meditation) is defined as the yogi's mind single-pointedly (*ekāgra*) contemplating with constant compassion (*karuṇā*) the welfare of all beings—classified as lowest (*hīna*), middle (*majjhima*), and highest (*uttama*). The practitioner identifies the self with all beings ("THAT VERY BEING, THAT VERY I AM; THAT I AM, THAT IS ALL BEINGS"), recognizing that "ALL THINGS HAVE A SINGLE, UNDIVIDED NATURE," empowered through yoga.[^2] This aligns with Mahāyāna emphasis on universal compassion and non-dual awareness as meditative stabilization leading to *bodhi*.

It also appears briefly in *Kalpabuddha* (another Kawi Buddhist tantric text) as the *mudrā* (hand gesture or seal) of Lord Vajradharma (associated with Amitābha), alongside his *jñāna* of *pratyavekṣaṇa* (discerning wisdom).[^3]

**In tattva texts** (Old Javanese works on metaphysical principles/reality, often Śaiva-leaning like *Vr̥haspatitattva*, *Gaṇapatitattva*, *San Hyan Tattvajnana*), *dhyāna* appears as a yogic or vow-related practice but lacks the detailed expositions found in Buddhist texts. For instance, in *Vratisaṃsāna* (on vows and contents/*tuṣṭi*), it is one of seven forms of internal contentment (*ādyātmika-tuṣṭi*): *śivārcana*, *adhyāya*, *adhyāpaka*, *svādhyāya*, *vrata*, *dhyāna*, *yoga*.[^4] No extended definitions of *dhyāna* as meditation appear in the searched tattva works (*San Hyan Tattvajnana* focuses on *cetana*/*acetana* tattvas like Śivatattva/Māyātattva; *Vr̥haspatitattva* on property ethics). It likely denotes contemplative absorption in Śaiva contexts, akin to Pātañjala yoga, but the corpus provides no unique elaboration here.[^5]

The corpus (37 works) covers select Old Javanese Buddhist (*San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, *Kalpabuddha*) and tattva (*San Hyan Tattvajnana*, etc.) texts; exhaustive coverage of all traditions is absent.

[^1]: *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, ch. 1, [para p-e76e3c](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19/01/translation/#p-e76e3c).

[^2]: *San Hyan Kamahayanikan*, ch. 1, [para p-c54f77](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19/01/translation/#p-c54f77).

[^3]: *Kalpabuddha*, ch. 4, [para p-60c6c3](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/04/translation/#p-60c6c3).

[^4]: *Vratisasana*, ch. 1, [para p-84feba](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-vratisasana-d0fe75/01/translation/#p-84feba).

[^5]: No direct matches in *San Hyan Tattvajnana* ch. 1 ([full chapter](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/)); *Vr̥haspatitattva* ch. 1 has partial "dhy" but unrelated to meditation.
